headline: Shohei Ohtani Goes First Overall to North Correa, and Abbotsford-Colby Twins snagging Blake Snell at Pick 172 (ADP 98) Was the Steal of the Draft
subheadline: From Ohtani at #1 to a 74-pick Blake Snell heist, the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft had everything — genius, chaos, and at least three managers who will be explaining themselves at the trade deadline.
body:
# 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft Review
## *By Bill Simmons, The Ringer*

---

## **I. The War Room Opens**

You know that scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane is on the phone working seventeen angles at once while everyone else in the room is just trying to keep up? That's every fantasy draft, except instead of Brad Pitt you've got fourteen grown adults staring at spreadsheets at 11 AM on a Sunday in March, arguing about whether Blake Snell is "back" or whether selecting a 38-year-old Sandy Alcantara in round nine means you've fully lost the plot. Welcome to the 2026 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft — fourteen teams, 210 picks, and enough questionable decision-making to fill a three-part ESPN documentary. I was there for all of it. I have notes. I have opinions. I have no chill.

Here's what you need to know going in: this draft had legitimate genius (the **Canberra Wombats** quietly assembling a late-round pitching museum while everyone else panicked), genuine theft (that **Blake Snell** falling to pick 172 is the fantasy equivalent of finding a Honus Wagner card at a garage sale), and the kind of reaching that makes you physically wince (hi, **General Hospital**, who apparently drafted **Luis Robert Jr.** in round eight like it was round three and nobody told them). The overall vibe? Think *Ocean's Eleven* where a couple of teams actually pulled the heist, a few teams thought they were in *Ocean's Eleven* but were actually in *Ocean's Twelve* — the one nobody liked — and at least two teams were just vibing in the lobby.

The backdrop matters here: **Sugar Land Skeeters** are the defending champs, back-to-back top-three finishes, legitimate dynasty energy. **BaseballPro77** is the only other franchise with a ring and has consistently posted top-seven finishes. Everyone is gunning for both of them, and the draft board reflected that tension — aggressive reaches, bold steals, and more than a few "what are they doing?" moments that will either look brilliant in October or get roasted in the league GroupChat. Let's break it all down.

---

## **II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

**North Correa** held the first overall pick and did exactly what you'd do with it: took **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** — the most valuable fantasy asset on the planet when you slot him at Util and let his bat carry your lineup while **Hiroshima Radiation** grabs the pitching version eight rounds later. No drama, no controversy, no creativity required. Picks two through six followed the chalky path: **Athletic Park Dragons** grabbed **Aaron Judge**, **Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** took **Juan Soto** (who just signed with the Mets and is officially must-draft territory), **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** secured **José Ramírez** at four, **BaseballPro77** locked in **Bobby Witt Jr.** at five, and **Albuquerque Bombers** took **Ronald Acuña Jr.** at six — coming off a torn ACL, sure, but Acuña returning to full health is the single most exciting thing in fantasy baseball this side of Ohtani himself.

The real Round 1 storyline starts at pick seven, where **Hiroshima Radiation** went off-script and grabbed **Tarik Skubal** — the best starting pitcher in baseball right now — which is either a genius move or the kind of thing that gets you docked a spot in every mock draft going forward, depending on your league's pitching scarcity. **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** followed with **Paul Skenes** at eight (top-five pitcher, easy), **Buttville Skidmarks** grabbed **Elly De La Cruz** at nine (the electric shortstop who runs like he's being chased), **General Hospital** took **Kyle Tucker** (now a Dodger, which is just unfair) at ten, **Sugar Land Skeeters** nabbed **Garrett Crochet** at eleven, **Riverside Roughnecks** selected **Jackson Chourio** at twelve, **Canberra Wombats** took **Gunnar Henderson** at thirteen, and **Benchwarmers** closed Round 1 with **Julio Rodríguez** at fourteen. All defensible. No disasters. The chaos comes later.

---

## **III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa** | Pick #1
Coming off back-to-back finishes of 14th and 12th, **North Correa** needed a rebuild centerpiece, and **Ohtani (Batter)** is exactly that. The problems start in Round 2, where they grabbed **Mookie Betts** eleven picks early (a reach, though Betts is still an elite contributor) and then doubled down by taking **Chris Sale** in Round 3 at ten picks above ADP — which, look, I love a bounce-back Sale narrative as much as the next guy, but that's a *lot* of faith in a guy whose career has more plot twists than a Shonda Rhimes show. **Jack Flaherty** in Round 10 at thirty picks early is the third reach flag, though the **Jeff Hoffman** steal in Round 13 partially redeems the late-round work. The roster has upside but the pitching investments are fragile. **Grade: B-** — Ohtani saves you, but you're one Sale injury away from a very long summer.

**Athletic Park Dragons** | Pick #2
**Aaron Judge** is the perfect pick at two — no argument. The issue is what follows. **Cristopher Sánchez** in Round 2 is solid value, and **James Wood** in Round 3 is exactly the kind of ascending young outfielder you want stashed away. But the back half of this draft has three reach flags: **Willson Contreras** at seventeen picks early, **Dansby Swanson** at eighteen early, and **Gleyber Torres** at fourteen early. (Three reaches in three consecutive rounds feels less like a strategy and more like a panic attack.) Coming off a 14th-place finish in 2025 after a 6th in 2024, the **Dragons** need consistency, not a roster full of players drafted on vibes. **Grade: C+** — Judge and Wood are real, but the middle rounds feel like they drafted with their heart instead of their board.

**Wisconsin Rapids Reapers** | Pick #3
**Juan Soto** at three is a gift, full stop. **Bryan Woo** in Round 2 is an interesting bet on a young Seattle arm with real upside, and **Mason Miller** closing games in Round 4 gives you elite saves and strikeout rate. The **Andrés Muñoz** pick in Round 6 doubles down on the Seattle bullpen, which is either smart (two elite closers!) or redundant depending on your roster construction philosophy. **Brandon Woodruff** in Round 9 at eighteen picks early is the biggest gamble — coming back from injury, huge upside if healthy — and the **Matthew Boyd** reach in Round 13 at 23 picks early is puzzling. The **Taylor Ward** steal in Round 11 is legitimately nice late-round value. **Grade: B** — Soto anchors a solid but not spectacular roster. The **Reapers** needed to be more aggressive in the middle rounds.

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins** | Pick #4
The reigning champs (2024 title, top-six finish in 2025) came in with a plan, and the plan was apparently "draft everyone we like regardless of ADP." **José Ramírez** is perfect at four. **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 2 gives you speed and power at a premium position. **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 3 is elite — a monster bat who hit the board exactly where you'd want him. Then things get spicy: **George Springer** at pick 53, taken *36 picks early* (ADP 89), is the single biggest positional reach in the draft. And **Brandon Nimmo** in Round 9 at *38 picks early* is almost impressively aggressive. But — and this is the thing about the **Twins** — their **Blake Snell** steal at Round 13, pick 172, when ADP had him at 98, is one of the best late-round heists of the entire draft. If Snell is healthy and locked in at Dodger Stadium... come on. That's a potential ace for round-thirteen prices. **Grade: A-** — The reaches sting, but the Snell pickup and the top-three foundation make this a genuine contender.

**BaseballPro77** | Pick #5
Two-time champion. Consistent top-seven finisher. Knows what they're doing. **Bobby Witt Jr.** at five is elite — arguably the best shortstop in baseball right now. **Ketel Marte** in Round 2 is exactly the kind of high-floor, multi-position player you build around. **Logan Gilbert** and **George Kirby** in Rounds 3 and 4 give you a legit Seattle pitching two-punch that's underrated across the industry. **Austin Riley** in Round 5, **Vinnie Pasquantino** in Round 6 — smart, value-driven picks throughout. The **Luis Castillo** reach in Round 7 at 29 picks early is the one eyebrow-raiser, but Castillo is a legitimate ace talent. The **Mike Trout** round-13 flier is a classic "what if he's healthy?" dart throw. This roster is deep, balanced, and managed by someone who has done this before. **Grade: A** — The defending contender who drafted like a defending contender.

**Albuquerque Bombers** | Pick #6
**Acuña Jr.** at six is the highest-ceiling pick in the draft if he's fully healthy, and the **Bombers** have top-three history to back up the aggressive approach. **Francisco Lindor** in Round 2 pairs beautifully with Acuña. **Freddie Freeman** in Round 4 is a rock. But then we hit the reach parade: **Christian Yelich** at 24 picks early, **Zack Wheeler** at *32 picks early*, **Sandy Alcantara** at *38 picks early* (Alcantara is coming off Tommy John — thirty-eight picks!), and **Carlos Rodón** at 24 early. That's four injury-risk veterans drafted significantly above market value in the middle and late rounds. The **Jorge Polanco** steal at the very end is a nice consolation prize. This roster lives and dies by health. **Grade: B-** — The top of the draft is beautiful. The middle is a medical chart.

**Hiroshima Radiation** | Pick #7
Bold move grabbing **Tarik Skubal** at seven — the best pitcher in baseball is a legitimate first-round pick in any pitching-heavy format — and snagging the **Ohtani (Pitcher)** version in Round 8 is the most creative roster construction move of the entire draft. You're essentially getting two versions of the same superhuman. **Kyle Schwarber** in Round 2 gives you the most terrifying power-strikeout ratio in baseball. **Bryce Harper** in Round 3 at Wrigley Field (wait, Philly — but still elite) is a premium bat. **Roman Anthony** in Round 4 might be the most exciting young player in the draft. The **Nathan Eovaldi** reach in Round 9 is the one concern — Texas heat and Eovaldi's injury history are not a relaxing combination. **Grade: A-** — Two Ohtanis. I'm sorry, *two Ohtanis*. That alone makes this worth watching.

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** | Pick #8
Runner-up in 2025, top-five in 2024 and 2023 — the **Wonderdogs** are the most consistently excellent team in the league that hasn't won a championship yet (the New England Patriots of this league, except without the rings). **Paul Skenes** at eight is a top-ten pick. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in Round 2 pairs with Skenes to give you arguably the best young pitching duo in the draft. And **Pete Alonso** in Round 3 at pick 36, when ADP had him at 22 — that's a genuine steal of a power bat who's coming off a monster year. The **Josh Hader** steal in Round 10 quietly gives you one of the most dominant closers in the game at deep-round prices. This team is built to win now. **Grade: A** — The most complete roster in the draft. The championship window is fully open.

**Buttville Skidmarks** | Pick #9
**Elly De La Cruz** at nine is exactly right — the most electrifying player in baseball when he's on. **Junior Caminero** in Round 2 is a high-upside young bat that could look like a steal by August. **Wyatt Langford** in Round 3 continues the youth movement. **Rafael Devers** in Round 4 gives you the thump. The concerns: **MacKenzie Gore** reach in Round 10 at 17 picks early, and **Bryan Reynolds** reach in Round 12 at 21 picks early. Two reaches in the same draft isn't fatal, but combined with a consistent 8th-place finish pattern, the **Skidmarks** need these picks to hit to break through. **Spencer Strider** in Round 11 is a massive swing — if he's back, that's round-three value in round eleven. **Grade: B** — More upside than their finishing history suggests. This could be the year.

**General Hospital** | Pick #10
The **General Hospital** experience is watching someone who has finished 13th and 13th in back-to-back years walk into a draft and immediately take **Kyle Tucker** at ten (correct!) and **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** in Round 2 (very correct!). Then in Round 8, they took **Luis Robert Jr.** at pick 103 when his ADP was 149 — *46 picks early*, which is the single largest reach of the entire draft. Robert is a talented but injury-riddled outfielder who belongs in rounds 10-12 given his health history. **Manny Machado** in Round 3 and **Max Fried** in Round 4 are solid foundational picks. The prospect pipeline of **Cam Schlittler** and **Bubba Chandler** is interesting. But two straight last-place finishes have a way of making managers reach, and you can see it happening in real time here. **Grade: C** — Great top five picks, one historically aggressive reach, and a roster that could go anywhere from 6th to 13th.

**Sugar Land Skeeters** | Pick #11
Defending champions. History of legendary steals. And they did it again. **Garrett Crochet** at eleven is top-ten pitcher value. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** in Round 2 is the high-risk, high-reward pick of the draft — when Tatis is locked in, there's no more exciting fantasy player alive. **Matt Olson** in Round 4 at pick 46, when ADP had him at 34 — a 12-pick steal of a perennial 35-homer, 100-RBI first baseman. **Edwin Díaz** in Round 6, falling 20 picks from ADP, is exactly the kind of high-strikeout closer the **Skeeters** have been mining for years. And **Ryan Helsley** in Round 11 at 14 picks below ADP continues the pattern. The **Max Muncy** reach at 30 picks early in Round 12 is the one head-scratcher, but with three legitimate closer steals and Olson anchoring the lineup, this team is constructed to repeat. **Grade: A** — When you're this good at finding value in rounds 4-11, winning championships becomes a habit.

**Riverside Roughnecks** | Pick #12
**Jackson Chourio** at twelve is quality value — one of the most exciting young outfielders in baseball coming off a breakout season. **Nick Kurtz** in Round 2 is a fascinating first-base prospect with massive power upside. The problems: **Cole Ragans** in Round 3 at 14 picks early, and **Trevor Story** in Round 7 at *25 picks early* — Story has played fewer than 100 games in multiple recent seasons, and reaching that aggressively for a fragile shortstop is the kind of swing that looks bad before Opening Day. The late-round redemption arc via **Carlos Estévez** (stolen 51 picks below ADP!) and **Kenley Jansen** (stolen 22 picks below ADP) shows there's a canny operator somewhere in that war room who just needs to get to the phone earlier. **Grade: C+** — Great core, shaky middle rounds, excellent late-game value. Perennial .500 team energy.

**Canberra Wombats** | Pick #13
The **Wombats** are the most fascinating team in this league. Top-three finishes in 2023 and 2025, disasters in 2024 — and a reputation for finding talent nobody else wants. **Gunnar Henderson** at thirteen is excellent value at a premium position. **Cal Raleigh** in Round 2 gives you one of the best fantasy catchers available. **Logan Webb** in Round 3 is a workhorse arm. But the steals are what define this roster: **Jacob deGrom** in Round 5 at thirteen picks below ADP (if he's healthy, that's a top-fifteen pick), **Jose Altuve** in Round 9 at *27 picks below ADP*, **Jo Adell** in Round 12 at 26 below, and **Aaron Nola** in Round 15 at 17 below. Four legitimate steals in a single draft is either brilliant scouting or dumb luck — and based on this team's history, it's the former. **Grade: A-** — The steal machine strikes again. DeGrom's health is the only variable.

**Benchwarmers** | Pick #14
Picking 14th gets you the turn — picks 14 and 15 back-to-back — and the **Benchwarmers** used it beautifully. **Julio Rodríguez** at fourteen and **Corbin Carroll** at fifteen is an outfield foundation that any team in this draft would take. **Zach Neto** in Round 3 and **Brent Rooker** in Round 4 continue the theme of underrated athleticism. The **Cody Bellinger** steal in Round 5 at 13 picks below ADP — now in pinstripes, motivated, healthy — could be one of the sneaky best picks of the entire draft. The **Emilio Pagán** steal in Round 14 at 23 below ADP is classic **Benchwarmers** late-round maneuvering, building on their history of finding that kind of value. A team that's gone 14th, 7th, 9th — trending upward with real talent now. **Grade: B+** — The turn was kind, the late rounds were sharp, and Bellinger in New York is a fascinating wildcard.

---

## **IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**The Three Best Steals:**

1. **Blake Snell**, Round 13, Pick 172, **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — ADP 98, fallen 74 picks. I don't care what anybody says: a Cy Young winner on the Dodgers falling to round thirteen is the most absurd value in this draft. If Snell is healthy and right, that's a top-fifteen pitcher for round-thirteen prices. The steal of the draft, full stop.

2. **Carlos Estévez**, Round 13, Pick 180, **Riverside Roughnecks** — ADP 129, fallen 51 picks. Elite closers don't fall this far. Whoever waited out the room to grab Estévez deserves a slow clap.

3. **Jose Altuve**, Round 9, Pick 125, **Canberra Wombats** — ADP 98, fallen 27 picks. Altuve is still Altuve. This slipping to round nine is inexcusable for every other team and a gift for the **Wombats**.

**The Three Worst Reaches:**

1. **Luis Robert Jr.**, Round 8, Pick 103, **General Hospital** — ADP 149, taken 46 picks early. The single largest reach of the draft, and it's not particularly close. Robert is exciting but chronically unavailable. This is a round-twelve pick being drafted in round eight.

2. **Brandon Nimmo**, Round 9, Pick 116, **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** — ADP 154, taken 38 picks early. The **Twins** can afford it given the rest of their draft, but Nimmo at ADP 154 being taken at 116 in a 14-team draft is hard to justify.

3. **Sandy Alcantara**, Round 9, Pick 118, **Albuquerque Bombers** — ADP 156, taken 38 picks early. Coming back from Tommy John surgery, in Miami, at 38 picks above market. Bold. Concerning. Very on-brand for the **Bombers**.

---

## **V. Five Bold Predictions for 2026**

1. **Blake Snell** becomes the best pitcher on the **Abbotsford-Colby Twins** by July, and the defending champs make a legitimate title run. The Snell steal and the Yordan Alvarez foundation make this roster scary when healthy.

2. **Hiroshima Radiation's** two-Ohtani experiment either wins them the championship or falls apart when the pitcher version gets shut down mid-season — there is no middle outcome here. Zero.

3. **General Hospital** finishes outside the top ten for the third straight year, and the **Luis Robert Jr.** round-eight reach becomes the most-discussed pick in the league GroupChat by Memorial Day.

4. **Sugar Land Skeeters** win their second consecutive title, driven by the **Edwin Díaz** and **Ryan Helsley** closer combination that literally nobody else in the draft valued correctly.

5. **Canberra Wombats** finish in the top three again, and **Jacob deGrom** quietly becomes the most talked-about steal in the league — assuming the knee holds, which is the most loaded assumption in baseball.

---

## **VI. The Pick: Champion Crowned**

Look, I've watched enough of these drafts to know that the team with the most balanced roster — elite anchor, pitching depth, saves coverage, and smart late-round value — almost always wins. And that team is **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs**.

**Paul Skenes** and **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** as your one-two pitching punch. **Pete Alonso** stolen in Round 3. **Josh Hader** quietly snagged in Round 10. **Michael Harris II** and **Ozzie Albies** filling out the lineup. **Framber Valdez** as a mid-round innings anchor. This team has no obvious holes — which is rare — and is managed by a franchise that has finished second, fifth, and second in the last three seasons.

The **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** are the 2026 Warriors: the most talented team on paper, the most consistent operation in the league, and a group that has been knocking on the championship door long enough that this feels like the year the door finally comes off the hinges. The **Skeeters** will compete. The **Twins** will be dangerous. **BaseballPro77** is always lurking.

But the **Wonderdogs** built something special in this draft — and this time, there's nothing left to wait for.

*The war room is closed. The season is coming. Let's ride.*
